deep_snippets.text: ## Chapter 15 Genes and Proteins 15.1 The Genetic Code 15.2 Prokaryotic Transcription 15.3 Eukaryotic Transcription 15.4 RNA Processing in Eukaryotes 15.5 Ribosomes and Protein Synthesis Since the rediscovery of Mendel’s work in 1900, the definition of the gene has progressed from an abstract unit of heredity to a tangible molecular entity capable of replication, expression, and mutation (Figure 15.1). Genes are composed of DNA and are linearly arranged on chromosomes. Genes specify the sequences of amino acids, which are the building blocks of proteins. In turn, proteins are responsible for orchestrating nearly every function of the cell. Both genes and the proteins they encode are absolutely essential to life as we know it. 15.1 The Genetic Code

By the end of this section, you will be able to do the following: • Explain systems biology • Describe a proteome • Define protein signature Proteins are the final products of genes, which help perform the function that the gene encodes. Amino acids comprise proteins and play important roles in the cell. All enzymes (except ribozymes) are proteins that act as catalysts to affect the rate of reactions. Proteins are also regulatory molecules, and some are hormones. Transport proteins, such as hemoglobin, help transport oxygen to various organs. Antibodies that defend against foreign particles are also proteins. In the diseased state, protein function can be impaired because of changes at the genetic level or because of direct impact on a specific protein. A proteome is the entire set of proteins that a cell type produces. We can study proteoms using the knowledge of genomes because genes code for mRNAs, and the mRNAs encode proteins. Although mRNA analysis is a step in the right direction, not all mRNAs are translated into proteins. Proteomics is the study of proteomes' function. Proteomics complements genomics and is useful when scientists want to test their hypotheses that they based on genes. Even though all multicellular organisms' cells have the same set of genes, the set of proteins produced in different tissues is different and dependent on gene expression. Thus, the genome is constant, but the proteome varies and is dynamic within an organism. In addition, RNAs can be alternately spliced (cut and pasted to create novel combinations and novel proteins) and many protein

Genes are composed of DNA and are linearly arranged on chromosomes. Genes specify the sequences of amino acids, which are the building blocks of proteins. In turn, proteins are responsible for orchestrating nearly every function of the cell. Both genes and the proteins they encode are absolutely essential to life as we know it. 15.1 The Genetic Code

supplements.curriculum_md_raw:
# 从基因到蛋白质

## 基本信息
- **学科**: 生物学
- **学段**: high
- **年级**: 10
- **章节/领域**: 基因的分子基础
- **内容来源**: TeachAny 课标知识点卫星文件

## 一、课标原文

### 1.1 内容要求
> 概述DNA分子上的遗传信息通过RNA指导蛋白质的合成
> 概述多数生物的基因是DNA分子的功能片段，有些病毒的基因在RNA分子上
> 概述DNA分子是由四种脱氧核苷酸构成，通常由两条碱基互补配对的反向平行长链形成双螺旋结构，碱基的排列顺序编码了遗传信息

## 二、教学目标

- 理解「从基因到蛋白质」的核心概念、方法与应用场景。
- 能结合材料、问题或真实情境解释「从基因到蛋白质」相关现象。
- 能用规范的学科语言表达思路、依据和结论。

## 三、教学重难点

- 教学重点：建立对「从基因到蛋白质」核心概念和基本方法的准确理解。
- 教学难点：把知识迁移到新的问题、材料或真实情境中。

## 五、教学建议

### 5.1 教学方法
- 以结构与功能、生命过程或生态关系为主线组织「从基因到蛋白质」教学。
- 通过图示、模型、实验或真实案例，引导学生解释生命现象背后的机制。
- 围绕课标要点“概述DNA分子上的遗传信息通过RNA指导蛋白质的合成”，设计观察记录、证据解释和生活应用任务。

### 5.2 学习活动设计
1. 以结构与功能、生命过程或生态关系为主线组织「从基因到蛋白质」教学。
2. 通过图示、模型、实验或真实案例，引导学生解释生命现象背后的机制。
3. 围绕课标要点“概述DNA分子上的遗传信息通过RNA指导蛋白质的合成”，设计观察记录、证据解释和生活应用任务。

## 七、课件制作参考

- 以结构与功能、生命过程或生态关系为主线组织「从基因到蛋白质」教学。
- 通过图示、模型、实验或真实案例，引导学生解释生命现象背后的机制。
- 围绕课标要点“概述DNA分子上的遗传信息通过RNA指导蛋白质的合成”，设计观察记录、证据解释和生活应用任务。

## 八、相关知识扩展

- 联系健康、生态、农业或生物技术案例，讨论「从基因到蛋白质」与现实生活的关系。
- 通过模型图、显微图或调查资料拓展学生对生命系统的理解。
supplements.textbook_summary:
## 教材化讲解参考（由课标要点生成）

### 核心概念
从基因到蛋白质是生物学学习中的重要知识点。教学时应围绕课标要点“概述DNA分子上的遗传信息通过RNA指导蛋白质的合成”展开，先明确概念边界，再通过例子、图示、实验、语篇或案例帮助学生建立可迁移的理解。

### 学习路径
1. 情境导入：以结构与功能、生命过程或生态关系为主线组织「从基因到蛋白质」教学。
2. 探究建构：通过图示、模型、实验或真实案例，引导学生解释生命现象背后的机制。
3. 应用迁移：围绕课标要点“概述DNA分子上的遗传信息通过RNA指导蛋白质的合成”，设计观察记录、证据解释和生活应用任务。

### 例题设计方向
可设计一道“识别核心概念”的基础题，一道“解释现象或文本材料”的理解题，一道“迁移到真实情境”的应用题，并要求学生说明依据。

deep_textbook_snippets.text: ## Chapter 15 Genes and Proteins 15.1 The Genetic Code 15.2 Prokaryotic Transcription 15.3 Eukaryotic Transcription 15.4 RNA Processing in Eukaryotes 15.5 Ribosomes and Protein Synthesis Since the rediscovery of Mendel’s work in 1900, the definition of the gene has progressed from an abstract unit of heredity to a tangible molecular entity capable of replication, expression, and mutation (Figure 15.1). Genes are composed of DNA and are linearly arranged on chromosomes. Genes specify the sequences of amino acids, which are the building blocks of proteins. In turn, proteins are responsible for orchestrating nearly every function of the cell. Both genes and the proteins they encode are absolutely essential to life as we know it. 15.1 The Genetic Code

By the end of this section, you will be able to do the following: • Explain systems biology • Describe a proteome • Define protein signature Proteins are the final products of genes, which help perform the function that the gene encodes. Amino acids comprise proteins and play important roles in the cell. All enzymes (except ribozymes) are proteins that act as catalysts to affect the rate of reactions. Proteins are also regulatory molecules, and some are hormones. Transport proteins, such as hemoglobin, help transport oxygen to various organs. Antibodies that defend against foreign particles are also proteins. In the diseased state, protein function can be impaired because of changes at the genetic level or because of direct impact on a specific protein. A proteome is the entire set of proteins that a cell type produces. We can study proteoms using the knowledge of genomes because genes code for mRNAs, and the mRNAs encode proteins. Although mRNA analysis is a step in the right direction, not all mRNAs are translated into proteins. Proteomics is the study of proteomes' function. Proteomics complements genomics and is useful when scientists want to test their hypotheses that they based on genes. Even though all multicellular organisms' cells have the same set of genes, the set of proteins produced in different tissues is different and dependent on gene expression. Thus, the genome is constant, but the proteome varies and is dynamic within an organism. In addition, RNAs can be alternately spliced (cut and pasted to create novel combinations and novel proteins) and many protein

Genes are composed of DNA and are linearly arranged on chromosomes. Genes specify the sequences of amino acids, which are the building blocks of proteins. In turn, proteins are responsible for orchestrating nearly every function of the cell. Both genes and the proteins they encode are absolutely essential to life as we know it. 15.1 The Genetic Code

An additional study that swapped the pups at birth (i.e., rats born to inattentive mothers grew up with attentive mothers and vice versa) showed the same positive effect of attentive mothering. How do genetics and/or epigenetics explain the results of this study? 29. Some autoimmune diseases show a positive correlation with dramatically decreased expression of histone deacetylase 9 (HDAC9, an enzyme that removes acetyl groups from histones). Why would the decreased expression of HDAC9 cause immune cells to produce inflammatory genes at inappropriate times? 30. A mutation within the promoter region can alter transcription of a gene. Describe how this can happen. 31. What could happen if a cell had too much of an activating transcription factor present? 32. A scientist identifies a potential transcription regulation site 300bp downstream of a gene and hypothesizes that it is a repressor. What experiment (with results) could he perform to support this hypothesis? 33. Describe how RBPs can prevent miRNAs from degrading an RNA molecule. 34. How can external stimuli alter posttranscriptional control of gene expression? 35. Protein modification can alter gene expression in many ways. Describe how phosphorylation of proteins can alter gene expression. 36. Alternative forms of a protein can be beneficial or harmful to a cell. errors.description: 把名词当结论，不结合结构与功能
忽视实验证据与变量控制

exercises.stem: 依据课标「从基因到蛋白质」：概述DNA分子上的遗传信息通过RNA指导蛋白质的合成 请简要阐述并举例。
exercises.answer: 应答覆盖课标要点，体现掌握与应用。

exercises.stem: 比较说明：概述多数生物的基因是DNA分子的功能片段，有些病毒的基因在RNA分子上
exercises.answer: 需写出关键概念、过程或方法，避免只背结论。

real_world: 观察人体健康、植物生长、生态环境或食品生产中与「从基因到蛋白质」相关的生命现象。
